content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

message.content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the prompt.md file to understand the exercise.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
            "properties": {
              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

The instructions are explicit and binding: I must use the TDD agents for each phase, running autonomously. This is a sequential, discipline-enforcing workflow — not a fan-out parallel one. The agents must be invoked in strict order (test-list → red → green → refactor per test), with each phase depending on the prior. input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office — premium quoting and claim processing for a magical-items insurance system, exposed as a `claim-office` CLI.

Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Requirements (from the kata spec in prompt.md):

ITEM VALUES / BASE PREMIUMS:
- Sword: insurance value 1000 G, base premium 100 G
- Amulet: 600 / 60
- Staff: 800 / 80
- Potion: 400 / 40
- Components (rune, moonstone): insured at 250 G each, base premium 25 G per component.
- A "building block" of exactly 3 ALIKE components (same type) costs 60 G base premium instead of 75 G. Block requires exactly 3. Examples: 2 runes→50, 3 runes→60, 4 runes→100, 7 runes→175 (one block of 3 = 60, plus 4×25=100 → 160... actually 7 runes = 175 per spec: blocks apply greedily? 7 = block(3)=60 + 4 runes... no. Per spec 7 runes→175. Re-derive: spec says 7 runes → 175 G. NOTE for derivation during tests.). 2 runes+1 moonstone→75 (no block, different types). 3 runes+3 moonstones→120 (two separate blocks).

PREMIUM MODIFIERS:
- Cursed item: +50% surcharge on that item's base premium (item-specific).
- High enchantment (level ≥ 5): +30% surcharge on that item's base premium (item-specific).
- Long-standing customer (yearsWithMHPCO ≥ 2): 20% loyalty discount (policy-wide).
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge — each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of customer history (applied to policy base premium).
- Follow-up contract: 15% discount on each contract after the customer's first quote in the scenario (policy-wide).
- 5 G processing fee added to every premium at the very end.
- Item-specific modifiers apply to the base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up) apply to the policy base premium (sum of all item base premiums); fee added last.
- Premiums rounded to whole G in MHPCO's favor (round UP).
- Empty item list → premium 5 G (fee only).

CLAIM PROCESSING:
- Deductible 100 G per damage event (per damaged item entry).
- Total payout per policy capped at 2× insurance sum (based on unmodified insurance values).
- Damage to items with enchantment ≥ 8 reimbursed at 50% of damage amount (then deductible).
- Damage to dragon-material items fully reimbursed (then deductible).
- If both high-enchantment(≥8) and dragon clauses apply, the 50% rule wins.
- Components (runes/moonstones) have no enchantment/material → standard reimbursement.
- Standard: full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible.
- Cap is shared across successive claims; payout reduced to remaining cap.
- Payouts rounded DOWN (MHPCO's favor).

KEY INTEGRATION EXAMPLES:
- Newcomer (0 years) cursed steel sword ench 3 → premium 165 (100 base +50 curse +10 first = 160 +5 fee).
- 3-year customer's 2nd quote, cursed steel sword ench 7 → premium 160 (100 +50 curse +30 high −20 loyalty +10 first −15 follow-up = 155 +5 fee).
- Dragon sword ench 8 damage 1000 → payout 400 (50% then deductible).
- Dragon sword ench 5 damage 800 → payout 700 (full then deductible).
- Steel sword ench 9 damage 1000 → 400. Steel sword ench 3 damage 500 → 400. Rune damage 200 → 100.

ERROR CASES (CLI exits non-zero):
- Unknown item type in quote.
- Claim damage entry references item not in policy / unknown type.
- More damage entries of a type than policy covers.
- Negative damage amount.

CLI: src/cli.ts reads JSON scenario {customer:{yearsWithMHPCO}, steps:[...]} from stdin, writes {results:[...]} to stdout. quote→{premium}, claim→{payout, remainingCap}. Steps processed sequentially; claim.policy is zero-based index of the quote step.

Order tests simple→complex, base functionality only. Use it.todo(). Run `pnpm test` to verify the todo list is recognized.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

```
const isRune = (item) => item.type === "rune";  // extracted predicate

```
function quote(...) {

  const runeBasePremium = runeCount === BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : runeCount * BASE_PREMIUM.rune;
  const nonRuneBasePremium = items.filter((item) => !isRune(item)).reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
  return nonRuneBasePremium + runeBasePremium + POLICY_FEE;
}
```
- Constants: `0` (reduce seed) = 1
- Bindings: `items`, `item`(×2), `runeCount`, `sum`, `runeBasePremium`, `nonRuneBasePremium` ≈ 7
- Invocations: `filter`, `length`, `===`, `*`, `filter`, `isRune`, `!`, `reduce`, `+`, `BASE_PREMIUM[]`, `+`, `+` ≈ 12 → mass 24
- Conditionals: `?:` = 1 → mass 4
- Loops: `filter`, `filter`, `reduce` = 3 → mass 15
- **Subtotal: ~51**

- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): `items.filter(isRune)` and `filter((item) => !isRune(item))` read as domain language rather than raw string comparisons.

**Deliberately NOT done (would add behavior tests don't demand):**

- Did **not** merge the two filter passes into a single partition reduce. That would lower mass slightly but obscures the two distinct concepts (count-of-runes vs sum-of-non-runes) before the block rule's true shape is known from upcoming tests.

**Mass Change:** ~51 → ~58 (Δ +7). APP rose slightly; accepted because Rule 2 and Rule 3 outrank Rule 4/APP in the priority ordering, and the change removes duplicated domain knowledge.

content.text:
All 12 tests still green.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The extraction moved the inline lookup `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (1 index invocation) out of the reduce callback into the named `basePremiumFor`, replacing it with a single named call. Net mass within `quote` is essentially unchanged (~50 → ~50): one index invocation became one function invocation. The change is a **Rule 2 (reveals intent)** improvement, not an APP reduction — and per the guidelines, Rule 2 trumps APP.

### Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted `basePremiumFor(item)` — names the "look up an item's base premium" concept that was an anonymous index expression inside the reduce. This is the same lookup the upcoming modifier/block tests will reuse, so giving it a name now improves readability without committing to future behavior.
- Mass: ~unchanged (one index invocation replaced by one named invocation). Rule 2 takes precedence over a mass delta here.

**Refactoring Considered but Deliberately NOT Applied (documented per the prompt's request):**

The flagged **asymmetry** between rune and moonstone handling is real:
- Runes: special-cased — counted, then block pricing applied (`runeCount === BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : ...`).
- Moonstones (and all other types): summed flat via the `nonRuneBasePremium` reduce, with no block logic, despite `moonstone: 25` now sitting in `BASE_PREMIUM` next to `rune: 25`.

This produces a **conceptual duplication** waiting to emerge: "form a block of 3 alike components, else charge per-item" is currently hard-coded to one type (rune). The clean form is to **group items by type** and apply block pricing per group. That single-pass, group-by-type restructuring would:
1. Eliminate the double iteration over `items` (the Rule 3 duplication), and
2. Make rune/moonstone handling symmetric.

I did **not** perform it because the very next test — "should form two separate blocks for 3 runes + 3 moonstones (120 base + 5 fee = 125 G)" — is what should drive that generalization. Restructuring now would be implementing ahead of the tests (a TDD violation) and would add the grouping abstraction before a test demands it (violating Rule 4: fewest elements / premature abstraction). The current rune-specific code is the honest minimal solution for the 12 passing tests.

I also did **not** rename `runeBasePremium`/`nonRuneBasePremium` to something more general (e.g., `blockPremium`/`flatPremium`), because the names accurately describe the current rune-only behavior; renaming them to imply generality would mislead until the moonstone-block test exists.

### Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `basePremiumFor(item)` helper to reveal the base-premium-lookup intent (Rule 2).
- **Mass Change**: ~50 → ~50 (negligible; the change is a clarity improvement, and Rule 2 trumps APP).
- **Tests**: All 12 passing ✅ (26 todo).
- **Documented asymmetry**: rune vs. moonstone handling is intentionally still asymmetric; the next test (3 runes + 3 moonstones → two blocks) will drive generalizing block pricing to group-by-type and collapse the double iteration. Not done now to avoid implementing ahead of tests.

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.

```

return sum + componentGroupPremium(type, count);

- Bindings: COMPONENT_TYPES, sum, type, count, items, item = 6

- Conditionals: 0 (moved out) → 0
- Loops: reduce, filter = 2 → 10

- Conditionals: `? :` = 1 → 4
- Mass: 2 + 6 + 4 = **12**

content.text:
This explains the discrepancy. There **is** a TypeScript type error (TS2353 — exactly as predicted) when running `tsc --noEmit`, but the test runner (`pnpm test` → `vitest run`) uses esbuild transform-only compilation that strips types **without type-checking**. So the test execution never sees a compilation error — it goes directly to the runtime assertion error.

My compilation prediction was correct about the *type system*, but incorrect about the *test run behavior*: the test-running pipeline does not fail on compilation, it proceeds to runtime. Since the Red phase verification is driven by `pnpm test`, I must report the compilation prediction as incorrect for the test-run outcome.

## Step 4 / 5 / 6 collapsed by the runner

Because `pnpm test` skips type-checking, there was no separate "create empty function" stage — the test went directly to a runtime assertion failure on the first run. The runtime error matches my runtime expectation exactly.

## Runtime Prediction (as it actually played out)

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should add 50% curse surcharge to the cursed item's base premium"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 155 (100 base + 50 curse surcharge + 5 fee)
- Actual value: 105 (cursed flag ignored: 100 base + 5 fee)
- Diff:
  Expected: 155
  Received: 105
```

This matches the observed output exactly: `expected 105 to be 155`.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should add 50% curse surcharge to the cursed item's base premium"
**Compilation Prediction**: "Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'cursed' does not exist in type '{ type: string; }'" (TS2353) ❌ Incorrect
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 155, received 105 ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

### Honest note on the Incorrect compilation prediction

The discrepancy is real and worth surfacing for the Green phase:

- **What I predicted:** The object literal `{ type: "sword", cursed: true }` would cause a compilation error (excess-property check, TS2353), because the parameter type is `{ type: string }[]`.
- **What actually happened under `pnpm test`:** No compilation error. The test ran straight to the runtime assertion (`expected 105 to be 155`). The `vitest`/esbuild pipeline transpiles by stripping types **without type-checking**, so the type error never blocks execution.
- **Confirmation the type error genuinely exists:** Running `npx tsc --noEmit` does report exactly the predicted error: `src/claim-office.spec.ts(87,36): error TS2353: Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'cursed' does not exist in type '{ type: string; }'.`

So the prediction was conceptually right about TypeScript's view but wrong about the Red-phase verification mechanism (`pnpm test`), which does not enforce type-checking. The test now fails for the right *runtime* reason (the cursed flag is ignored).

### Items the Green phase must address

- Widen the item type to include an optional `cursed?: boolean` (in `claim-office.ts`), both to satisfy `tsc` and to read the flag.
- Apply a 50% surcharge to a cursed item's own base premium so `quote([{ type: "sword", cursed: true }], ...)` returns `155`.

- Test: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts` (line 86–88, activated test)

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Refactored `mainItemsPremium` block:
```
const mainItemsPremium = items
  .filter((item) => !isComponent(item))
  .reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemPremium(item), 0);
```
- Constants: `0` (seed) = 1
- Bindings: `mainItemsPremium`, `item`(filter), `sum`, `item`(reduce) = 4
- Invocations: `filter`, `isComponent`, `reduce`, `+`, `itemPremium` = 5 → mass 10
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: `filter`, `reduce` = 2 → mass 10
- Assignments: 0

Block mass: 1 + 4 + 10 + 10 = **25** (down from ~36).

The extracted `itemPremium` helper carries the moved mass (constants 2, bindings 3, invocations `basePremiumFor`/`*`/`+` = 6, conditional 4 ≈ 15). Total system mass is roughly unchanged — the goal here was clarity and a single extension point, not raw reduction. The `quote` body's local mass dropped ~30%, which is the meaningful win since `quote` is the orchestration function readers scan first.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming (Rule 2): Renamed `mainBasePremium` to `mainItemsPremium`. After the curse surcharge was added, the value is no longer a pure "base" premium; the new name reveals it is the full premium for the main items.
- Intent / future-proofing (Rule 2 + Rule 3): Extracted `itemPremium(item)` — names the concept "premium for one main item including its item-specific surcharges" and gives a single home where the upcoming high-enchantment surcharge will stack, rather than growing an inline ternary chain inside the reduce.
- The reduce body collapsed from a 4-line block to a single expression, lowering the local APP mass of `quote` (~36 to ~25 for that block).

**Constraint honored:** No new behavior added. `itemPremium` computes exactly base + curse surcharge, identical to before. The enchantment surcharge (next todo) is intentionally NOT implemented.

## Step 7: Completion

content.text:
No build script exists; vitest does type-aware transforms and all tests pass green, confirming compilation.

## Step 5: New APP Mass

By extracting `loyaltyDiscountFor`, the `quote` body shed the inline `loyaltyDiscount` assignment (assign:6) and its ternary/comparison/multiply chain (~15 mass), replaced by a single invocation (invoke:2 + bindings:2 = ~4). The extracted helper carries that logic at the same intrinsic mass it had before. So total program mass is roughly unchanged (~+1 for the new binding parameter), but mass is **redistributed out of the `quote` orchestrator** into a focused, named function — the `quote` body dropped from ~96 to ~81.

This is the correct APP trade-off: Rule 2 (reveals intent) takes priority over raw mass minimization, and the localized mass of `quote` is meaningfully lower.

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Naming (Rule 2): Evaluated all names first — `quote`, `policyBasePremium`, `mainItemsPremium`, `componentBasePremium`, and the existing constants already revealed intent. No renames needed.
- Extract Function (Rule 2): Pulled the inline loyalty-discount arithmetic into `loyaltyDiscountFor(policyBasePremium, context)`. The `quote` function now reads as a clear pipeline: compute base premiums, subtract the named policy-wide discount, add fee.

**Deliberately NOT done (avoiding untested behavior):**
- The prompt suggested a possible policy-wide-modifier structure for the upcoming first-insurance (+10%) and follow-up (-15%) modifiers. I did **not** build it — those tests are still `it.todo()`. Building a generic modifier pipeline now would be premature abstraction (Rule 4 violation / TDD failure mode "implementing beyond what tests demand"). The new `loyaltyDiscountFor` helper is a natural, honest seam: when the next tests go red, the modifiers can be introduced incrementally without speculation.
- Named constants for the loyalty rate (0.2) and threshold (2) were already extracted in the green phase, so that suggested improvement was already satisfied.

**Rules check:**
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): 19/19 green before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): Improved — loyalty modifier now has a name.
- Rule 3 (No duplication): The two `reduce` sums operate on distinct domains; merging would hurt clarity. None removed.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): No unnecessary elements added; declined the speculative structure.

## Step 7: Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted inline loyalty-discount logic into a named `loyaltyDiscountFor` helper, revealing the policy-wide-modifier intent. Names and rate/threshold constants were already clean.
- **Mass Change**: `quote` body localized mass reduced (~96 → ~81); total program mass roughly unchanged. Rule 2 (clarity) prioritized over raw mass.
- **Tests**: All 19 passing ✅ (19 todo remain).

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
New helper firstInsuranceSurchargeFor: (policyBasePremium) =>
  policyBasePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE
- Bindings: policyBasePremium (param), policyBasePremium (use), FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 3
- Invocations: * = 1 = 2
Subtotal: 5

quote modifier section (after):
- Removed: firstInsuranceSurcharge binding line (was 3 bindings + 1 invocation*2 = 5)
- premium line now: bindings premium, policyBasePremium, POLICY_FEE = 3;
  invocations: -, loyaltyDiscountFor(), +, firstInsuranceSurchargeFor(), + = 5 = 10
- return Math.ceil: invocation 2 + binding 1 = 3
```

Net mass is essentially **flat** (the inline `* RATE` moved into the helper; the call site gained one invocation but lost the intermediate binding line). Mass change ≈ neutral (≈ 0). This is expected and acceptable.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Extracted `firstInsuranceSurchargeFor(policyBasePremium)` so it mirrors the existing `loyaltyDiscountFor(policyBasePremium, context)`. The two policy-wide modifiers now share a consistent named-helper shape, making it self-evident that they are sibling terms applied to the policy base premium.
- ✅ **Naming:** New helper name `firstInsuranceSurchargeFor` follows the established `…For(policyBasePremium)` convention; reuses the existing `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` constant (no new magic numbers).
- ✅ **Coherent organization:** The `quote` premium expression now reads as a clean sequence of policy-wide modifier terms — `base − loyalty + firstInsurance + fee` — providing a coherent home for the follow-up discount coming in the next test.

**Decisions deliberately NOT taken:**
- ❌ Did **not** collapse loyalty + first-insurance into one generic `policyModifier(rate, …)` helper. Although both are "rate × policyBasePremium" (Rule 3 duplication), loyalty is conditional on tenure while first-insurance is unconditional. A generic abstraction would obscure that difference. **Rule 2 trumps Rule 3** — distinct named helpers are clearer.
- ❌ Did **not** add any follow-up / `isFollowUp` behavior — that is the next (still `it.todo`) test, and adding it now would be untested behavior.

**Mass:** ≈ neutral (the inline surcharge term moved into a helper; the intermediate binding line was removed, offsetting the added call invocation). Accepted because the change is justified by clarity (Rule 2), not mass.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `firstInsuranceSurchargeFor` helper to mirror `loyaltyDiscountFor`, making the two policy-wide modifiers structurally symmetric and the `quote` premium expression coherent.
- **Mass Change:** ≈ neutral (clarity-motivated, not mass-motivated; Rule 2 over APP).
- **Tests:** All 20 passing ✅ (18 todo remaining).

- ❌ Did **not** collapse loyalty + first-insurance into one generic `policyModifier(rate, …)` helper. ## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Before** — single five-term `premium` expression:
- bindings (policyBasePremium ×4, context ×2, POLICY_FEE) ≈ 7
- invocations: 3 helper calls (×2 = 6) + 4 operators (`- + - +`, ×2 = 8) = 14
- Expression mass ≈ 21

**After** — `policyModifiers` (three-term) + `premium` (three-term):
- `policyModifiers`: bindings (policyBasePremium ×3, context ×2) = 5; invocations: 3 helper calls (6) + 3 operators (unary `-`, `+`, `-`) ≈ 6 = 12; total ≈ 17
- `premium`: bindings (policyBasePremium, policyModifiers, POLICY_FEE) = 3; invocations: 2 operators (×2 = 4); total ≈ 7
- Combined ≈ 24

**Mass Change**: ≈ 21 → ≈ 24 (Δ +3). One extra named binding (`policyModifiers`) adds a small amount of mass.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied** — Introduced a named `policyModifiers` intermediate:

- ✅ **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 21 tests green before and after.
- ✅ **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The final premium now reads as a three-part business statement — `policyBasePremium + policyModifiers + POLICY_FEE` (base, then the net of all policy-wide adjustments, then the fee). The reader no longer has to track four `+`/`-` signs interleaved across a five-term expression to see the overall shape.
- ✅ **Naming**: All four names kept; they already reveal intent precisely.
- ❌ **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Considered consolidating the three structurally-similar helpers (`applies ? base * rate : 0`) into one generic `modifierFor`. **Rejected** — this is incidental structural similarity, not knowledge duplication. Each helper encodes a distinct business rule (loyalty threshold, unconditional first-insurance, follow-up flag) with its own condition and rate. A generic helper would erase the named business vocabulary, trading Rule 2 clarity for Rule 3 DRY — forbidden by the priority order. The three helpers stay separate and parallel.
- **APP (Rule 4 / mass)**: Mass rose slightly (≈ +3) from the new binding. Per the standing guidance that Rule 2 trumps APP, the clarity gain justifies the minor mass increase. No unnecessary elements were added beyond the single explaining binding.

The consolidation the prompt invited was applied at the **call-site** (grouping the three modifiers into one named total) rather than at the **helper level** (merging the rules), preserving the distinct business rules while improving how the quote calculation reads.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted a named `policyModifiers` binding in `quote`, so the final premium reads as `base + modifiers + fee`. The three policy-wide helpers were deliberately left separate to preserve their distinct business meaning.
- **Mass Change**: ≈ 21 → ≈ 24 (Δ +3 for one explaining binding; accepted under Rule 2 > APP).
- **Tests**: All 21 passing ✅ (17 todo remain).

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass (the changed region)

**Before** (the `policyModifiers` + `premium` region):
- `policyModifiers`: binding 1; three function invocations 6; unary `-`, `+`, `-` = 3 operators ≈ 6; arg refs ~6 → ~19
- `premium`: binding 1; three `+` invocations 6; term refs 4 → ~11
- **Region subtotal ≈ 30**

**After:**
- `policyDiscounts`: binding 1; two function invocations 4; one `+` invocation 2; arg refs ~4 → ~11
- `policySurcharges`: binding 1; one function invocation 2; arg ref 1 → ~4
- `premium`: binding 1; four operators (`+ + - +`) = 4 invocations 8; term refs 5 → ~14
- **Region subtotal ≈ 29**

**Mass change: ~30 → ~29 (Δ ≈ −1).** Effectively mass-neutral. I added one binding (`policyDiscounts`/`policySurcharges` vs the single `policyModifiers`) but removed the unary-negation operator and made the operator count slightly lower. As intended, this refactoring was driven by **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**, not by APP — and per project guidance, clarity trumps mass.

- Extracted `payoutForDamage(damage)` — names the single-damage reimbursement concept, matching the `xFor` helper convention used throughout `quote`.

**`BASE_PREMIUM` / `INSURANCE_VALUE` consolidation (evaluated, rejected)**: These are parallel per-type lookups (Rule 3 — duplication). I deliberately did **not** merge them into a single record. They represent two independent domain concerns (premium pricing vs. insured value), can change independently, and merging would force every entry to carry both — coupling unrelated knowledge and reducing clarity. Rule 2 trumps Rule 3, and merging here is over-engineering with no behavioral benefit.

- `payoutForDamage` is now a single, branch-free formula: `amount * rate - deductible`. Its mass dropped (no conditionals).

**Refactoring Applied** — extracted `reimbursementRateFor(item)`:

- Rule 1 (Tests pass): All 28 green before and after; typecheck clean.

- Rule 3 (No duplication): No duplication existed; none introduced.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Added one function + one constant. Justified: this is the explicit **seam the next two tests need**. `payoutForDamage` itself shed a local variable and its conditional, becoming a flat formula.

**Naming**: `payoutForDamage`, `isHighEnchantment`, and the existing constants already revealed intent — no renames.

**Why this seam matters for what's coming** (without adding untested behavior): the dragon-material clause and the "50% wins over dragon" conflict test are both fundamentally about *which reimbursement rate applies*. They now have one obvious place to live — `reimbursementRateFor` — where the rate selection happens. The apply-rate-then-deduct mechanic in `payoutForDamage` will not need to change. No dragon logic was added; only the structure that will host it.

const r2 = claim([{type:"sword", enchantment:3}], {cause:"fire", damages:[{itemType:"sword", amount:1500}]}, r1.remainingCap);
expect(r2.payout).toBe(600); expect(r2.remainingCap).toBe(0);

My compilation error prediction was **incorrect**. The test ran without a TypeScript compilation error — Vitest (using esbuild for transpilation) strips types and does not type-check argument counts at test-run time. content.text:
No build script; vitest uses esbuild and would surface type-shape issues at runtime. The tests pass cleanly. The refactoring is complete.

## Refactor Phase Summary

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
All function names clearly reveal intent and follow consistent conventions (`assert*` for guards, `*For`/`*By*` for computations). No renames needed.

### Step 2: Refactoring Applied — Rule 3 (No Duplication) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)

I refactored `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`. The original iterated over **every damage entry** and, for each, re-filtered both the full `policy` array and the full `damages` array — performing the same per-type count repeatedly (redundant conceptual duplication and O(n²) work).

The refactor extracts a reusable `countByKey<T>` helper that tallies counts per type in a single pass, then iterates the resulting map once per distinct type.

### APP Mass Comparison (`assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` body)

Before:
- Loops: 3 (`for...of` + 2 `filter` re-run on every iteration) = 15
- Conditionals: 4 (`if` + `>` + 2 filter predicates)
- Invocations: filter x2, length x2, Error
- Approx total mass: ~33

After (helper + guard combined, but each runs each input once):
- Loops: `countByKey` reduce (shared, 1) + guard `for...of` (1) = 2 distinct loops, no per-iteration re-filtering = 10
- Conditionals: `if` + `>` + 2 `?? 0` nullish guards
- Invocations: `set`/`get`, `keyOf`
- Approx total mass: ~28, and the redundant repeated filtering is eliminated (real algorithmic improvement from O(damages × (policy + damages)) to O(policy + damages))

The mass is lower and, more significantly, the duplicated per-iteration counting work is gone.

### Rule 2 Check (Clarity trumps APP)
My first attempt reused a `{ type: string }` helper that forced a `.map(... => ({ type: ... }))` adapter at the damage call site — clever but obscured intent. I revised to a generic `countByKey<T>(items, keyOf)` so both call sites read declaratively (`countByKey(policy, item => item.type)` and `countByKey(damages, d => d.itemType)`), restoring clarity without the throwaway-object hack.

### Tests
All 37 tests passing, 1 todo (the negative-damage-amount test, intentionally left for the next Red phase as instructed).

### Files
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: Extracted generic `countByKey` helper; rewrote `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` to count once per type instead of re-filtering per damage entry (removed duplication + redundant iteration).
**Mass Change**: ~33 → ~28 in the guard, plus elimination of O(n²) repeated filtering.
**Tests**: All 37 passing (1 todo) ✅

Proceeding to the next test (negative damage amount) is the next step in the cycle.

- "The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`"

This is a hard requirement of the exercise. The TDD test list scoped it out as "a separate concern," but the exercise is not complete without it. The done marker was written prematurely. I need to implement the CLI.

The CLI must handle the scenario format: `{customer:{yearsWithMHPCO}, steps:[...]}`, process steps sequentially (quotes create policies; claims reference a policy by step index and share the cap across successive claims on the same policy), and produce `{results:[...]}`. It must also handle the error cases (exit non-zero, write to stderr).

Let me build the CLI via a TDD micro-cycle using the agents, since the rules mandate agents for TDD. But the CLI is integration glue (stdin/stdout/process). I'll write a focused spec for the CLI orchestration logic and implement it. Let me first re-read the implementation to understand the exact exported signatures the CLI must call.

The core is clean and complete. Now I need the CLI. The done marker was written prematurely — the CLI is a hard requirement of the exercise. The TDD rules govern the core logic; the CLI is integration glue (process/stdin/stdout wiring), but I should still drive its scenario-orchestration logic with tests via the agents to stay within the workflow discipline.

The CLI needs scenario orchestration: track per-quote-step policies and per-policy follow-up state and shared cap across successive claims. Key behaviors from the spec:
- `customer.yearsWithMHPCO` applies to all quotes.
- `isFollowUp` = true for every quote after the customer's first quote in the scenario.
- Claims reference policy by step index; successive claims on the same policy share the cap.
- Error cases exit non-zero, write to stderr, no results on stdout.

Let me drive this with a TDD cycle. I'll launch the test-list agent for the CLI orchestration logic, then red/green/refactor. First, let me update the done marker since work isn't actually done.

input.description: Test list for CLI scenario runner
input.prompt:
Feature: CLI scenario runner for the MHPCO Claim Office. A pure function runScenario(scenario) that orchestrates the existing quote() and claim() functions over a scenario document, to be wrapped by a thin src/cli.ts (stdin→stdout). The core quote/claim logic already exists and is fully tested (38 tests).

Test file: src/scenario.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/scenario.ts

The scenario runner is the ONLY new logic to test (the CLI stdin/stdout wiring itself is trivial glue and out of scope for the test list). 

Scenario input shape:
{
  customer: { yearsWithMHPCO: number },
  steps: Array<
    | { op: "quote", items: Item[] }
    | { op: "claim", policy: number /* zero-based index of the quote step */, incident: { cause: string, damages: Array<{itemType: string, amount: number}> } }
  >
}

runScenario(scenario) returns { results: Array<{premium:number} | {payout:number, remainingCap:number}> } — same length and order as steps.

Behavior to cover (derive the test list from these, ordered simple→complex):
1. A single quote step → results has one {premium}. The premium uses customer.yearsWithMHPCO and isFollowUp=false for the first quote. (e.g. amulet, yearsWithMHPCO 5 → premium computed by quote()).
2. Multiple quote steps: the FIRST quote in the scenario has isFollowUp=false; EVERY subsequent quote has isFollowUp=true (follow-up discount applies to contracts after the customer's first quote).
3. A claim step references a quote step by its zero-based index via the `policy` field; the claim uses the items from that quote step as the policy. Result is {payout, remainingCap}.
4. Successive claims against the SAME policy share the cap: the second claim on a policy starts from the remainingCap left by the first claim on that policy. (Track remaining cap per policy/quote-step index.)
5. Results array preserves order and length of steps (mix of quote and claim results interleaved).
6. The schema example from the prompt: customer yearsWithMHPCO 5, step0 quote one amulet, step1 claim policy 0 amulet damage 200 → results [{premium:...},{payout:...,remainingCap:...}].

DO NOT test the stdin/stdout process wiring or process.exit — those belong to cli.ts (out of scope). DO NOT add generic input-validation tests beyond what the scenario behavior requires. Focus on the orchestration: follow-up flagging, policy lookup by index, and shared cap across successive claims on the same policy.

Use it.todo(). Run pnpm test to confirm the todos are recognized (it should now show the 38 existing tests plus the new todos).

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
export function runScenario(scenario: Scenario): ScenarioResult {
  const { yearsWithMHPCO } = scenario.customer;
  const results = scenario.steps.map((step, index) => {
    const isFollowUp = scenario.steps.slice(0, index).some((prior) => prior.op === "quote");
    const premium = quote(step.items, { yearsWithMHPCO, isFollowUp });
    return { premium };
  });
  return { results };
}
```

| Component | Count | Unit | Subtotal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constants (`0`, `"quote"`) | 2 | 1 | 2 |
| Bindings (scenario, yearsWithMHPCO, results, step, index, isFollowUp, prior, premium) | 8 | 1 | 8 |
| Invocations (`.map`, `.slice`, `.some`, `quote`) | 4 | 2 | 8 |
| Conditionals (`prior.op === "quote"` comparison) | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| Loops (`.map`, `.some` iteration) | 2 | 5 | 10 |
| Assignments | 0 | 6 | 0 |

**Total Mass: 32**

**Mass Change: 24 → 32 (Δ +8)**

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent, plus Rule 4 cleanup):

- Eliminated the mutable `let hasQuotedBefore` flag and its in-`map` reassignment (a side-effecting closure — a smell).
- Derived `isFollowUp` directly from the step history: true only when an *earlier step was a quote* (`prior.op === "quote"`).
- The flag now means exactly what tracking it should mean. Previously it flipped on *every* step, so its name (`hasQuotedBefore`) was a lie that happened to be harmless only because all current steps are quotes.

**On the correctness tightening — decision: tighten now.**
I chose to make the quote-only tightening immediately rather than defer to the claim test. Rationale:
- It is a Rule-2 (reveal intent) fix, not a speculative feature. I added no claim handling; I only made the existing follow-up logic honest about what "first quote" means per the spec.
- It is fully test-covered: every step in all 40 tests is a quote, so quote-only and step-any are behaviorally identical today — all 40 stay green (verified).
- It pre-empts a latent defect: when claim steps arrive, claims must NOT count toward follow-up flagging. The old code would have mis-flagged a quote following a claim. The new code is already correct for that case.

**On APP mass — deliberately accepted the increase (24 → 32).**
Rule 2 trumps APP. The mass rose because deriving the flag re-scans prior steps (`.slice().some()` adds an invocation, a conditional, and a loop). I accepted this because:
- The alternative — keeping a mutable flag flipped only on quotes — would require `let` + a conditional reassignment inside the loop, which is both a side-effect smell and still carries comparable mass.
- The derived version is referentially transparent (no mutation), correct under the upcoming claim feature, and its name no longer lies. Clarity and correctness outweigh 8 mass units.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Replaced the mutable per-step `hasQuotedBefore` flag with a side-effect-free derivation; `isFollowUp` is now true only when an earlier step is a quote, making the follow-up logic honest about the spec's "first quote" meaning.
- **Mass Change**: 24 → 32 (Δ +8) — accepted deliberately; Rule 2 (reveals intent) and correctness outweigh APP.
- **Tests**: All 40 passing (4 todos remain). Verified green.

content.text:
## Step 3: Verify Compilation Error - PREDICTION WAS WRONG

My compilation error prediction was **Incorrect**. The test runner (`vitest run`) uses esbuild for transpilation, which strips TypeScript types without performing type checking. So the type error I predicted did not block execution. Instead, the code ran and threw a runtime `TypeError: items is not iterable`.

This is an honest discrepancy I must report: I predicted a TypeScript compilation error, but `pnpm test` (vitest) does not type-check at runtime, so the code executed and failed at runtime instead.

The actual failure is a **runtime error**: the claim step has no `items` property (it's `undefined`), so `quote(step.items, ...)` passes `undefined` into `assertKnownItemTypes`, where `for (const item of undefined)` throws `TypeError: items is not iterable`. This matches the runtime-error possibility described in the task prompt (it throws a TypeError when the claim step has no items).

Since the test already fails at runtime due to the existing implementation calling `quote(step.items, ...)` for every step, the two-stage process collapses: there is no separate "empty function" step needed (the function already exists). The runtime error is the meaningful failure.

## Step 5: Prediction - Runtime Error

The prediction I had implicitly made (and the task asked me to verify honestly): the claim step has no `items`, so `quote` is called with `undefined`, throwing a `TypeError`.

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should resolve a claim's policy from the referenced quote step and return payout and remainingCap"
- Expected: Runtime TypeError (the claim step has no `items`, so quote(undefined, ...) is called)
- Expected behavior: code throws "items is not iterable" (or "Cannot read ... of undefined") inside assertKnownItemTypes when iterating undefined
- Actual value: TypeError: items is not iterable thrown from claim-office.ts:66 via quote() called at scenario.ts:27
```

This is confirmed by the test run above. The test fails for the right reason: the implementation does not yet handle claim steps — it blindly calls `quote(step.items, ...)` for every step, and the claim step has no `items`.

## Step 7: Report Completion

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should resolve a claim's policy from the referenced quote step and return payout and remainingCap"
**Compilation Prediction**: Type '"claim"' is not assignable to type '"quote"' (object literal type error) ❌ Incorrect
**Runtime Prediction**: TypeError: items is not iterable (quote called with undefined items for claim step) ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with a runtime TypeError ("items is not iterable") thrown from assertKnownItemTypes via quote(step.items) at scenario.ts:27, because the implementation calls quote() for every step and the claim step has no items.

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

### Discrepancy Explanation (mandatory, since a prediction failed)

My **compilation prediction was Incorrect**. I predicted `pnpm test` would surface a TypeScript type error first. It did not, because `pnpm test` runs `vitest run`, which transpiles TypeScript via esbuild (type-stripping only, no type checking). The mismatched object literal therefore did not halt execution at the test stage. Note that the type mismatch is genuinely present and would be caught by `tsc`/IDE type checking — `ScenarioStep` is currently only `QuoteStep`, so `{op:"claim", policy:0, incident:{...}}` is not assignable. But the vitest-based test command never runs the type checker, so the program executed and failed at runtime instead.

The **runtime failure is the right reason** to be red: the implementation in `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/scenario.ts` (line 27) calls `quote(step.items, ...)` for every step, including the claim step which has no `items`, producing `TypeError: items is not iterable`.

- **Test file**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/scenario.spec.ts` (test on line 33 now active; 3 todos remain)

- **Expected behavior**: `runScenario` must distinguish step `op`. For a `quote` step → `{premium}` as before (115 here). For a `claim` step → resolve `policy` index to the items of that quote step, call `claim(policyItems, incident)`, and return `{payout, remainingCap}` (here payout 400, remainingCap 1600). The `ScenarioStep` union and result type also need extending to include the claim shape (`ClaimResult`).

This is an honest discrepancy I must report: I predicted a TypeScript compilation error, but `pnpm test` (vitest) does not type-check at runtime, so the code executed and failed at runtime instead.

The actual failure is a **runtime error**: the claim step has no `items` property (it's `undefined`), so `quote(step.items, ...)` passes `undefined` into `assertKnownItemTypes`, where `for (const item of undefined)` throws `TypeError: items is not iterable`. This matches the runtime-error possibility described in the task prompt (it throws a TypeError when the claim step has no items).

Since the test already fails at runtime due to the existing implementation calling `quote(step.items, ...)` for every step, the two-stage process collapses: there is no separate "empty function" step needed (the function already exists). The runtime error is the meaningful failure.

content.text:
All 41 tests pass and tsc --noEmit is now clean (it was failing before with 5 errors). Both verifications green.

Let me document the refactoring with the APP analysis.

## Refactor Phase Complete

### Step 1 — Naming Evaluation (first priority)
- `runScenario` — clearly reveals intent. **Kept.**
- The inline branches were anonymous; extracted to **`runQuoteStep`** and **`runClaimStep`** — names now state what each branch does.
- Added **`isQuoteStep`** type-guard predicate — name reveals both its boolean intent and its narrowing role.
- `policyStep`, `isFollowUp`, `yearsWithMHPCO` — already clear, kept.

### Step 2 — Refactorings applied

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + type-safe union access — the cast `as QuoteStep` was the core problem.**
- Before: `const policyStep = scenario.steps[step.policy] as QuoteStep;` — an unchecked assertion. If a claim's `policy` index pointed at another claim, `.items` would be accessed on the wrong shape silently.
- After: an `isQuoteStep(step): step is QuoteStep` type guard narrows the looked-up step. `.items` is now accessed only after the compiler has *proven* the step is a `QuoteStep`, with an explicit error if the index does not reference a quote step. No cast remains.

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass) — tsc cleanliness.** The previous `StepResult = QuoteResult | ClaimResult` union compiled internally but made the spec fail `tsc --noEmit` (5 errors: `.premium`/`.payout`/`.remainingCap` accessed on the bare union without narrowing). The spec is the contract and must not change, so `StepResult` is now a single shape with optional `premium?`/`payout?`/`remainingCap?`. The spec's positional property access compiles, and `claim()`/`{premium}` results still structurally satisfy it.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication) / structure** — the dispatch is now a single ternary delegating to two named helpers; `scenario.steps` is destructured once to `steps` instead of being repeated.

### Step 5 — APP Mass

| | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `runScenario` only | ~40 | ~22 (map + ternary + 2 invocations + slice) |
| Total (incl. extracted helpers) | ~40 | ~46 |

Local mass of `runScenario` dropped (logic moved out). Total mass rose slightly because the type guard and two helpers add invocations/bindings. This is the deliberate **Rule 2 > APP** trade-off: clarity and type-safety (eliminating the unsafe cast, naming each branch) justify the modest mass increase. Conditional count also dropped: the inline `prior.op === "quote"` comparison (mass 4) was replaced by reusing the `isQuoteStep` guard (a function reference passed to `.some`).

### Step 6 — Decision
Refactoring performed: replaced unsafe `as QuoteStep` cast with a verified `isQuoteStep` type guard; extracted `runQuoteStep`/`runClaimStep`; reshaped `StepResult` so the spec compiles under `strict`. Removed the unused `QuoteResult`/`ClaimResult` local aliases.

### Verification
- Tests: **41 passing**, 3 todo. Green.
- `tsc --noEmit`: **clean (exit 0)** — was 5 errors before.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/scenario.ts`

Proceeding to the next test.

`@types/node` isn't installed and isn't a declared dependency. The original project's `tsc` was clean for the core/scenario modules because they used no Node globals. The CLI inherently needs `process` and `stdin`. 

I shouldn't add a new dependency without need. Two clean options: (1) add `@types/node` as a dev dependency, or (2) declare minimal ambient types for the Node globals the CLI uses. Since the test runner (vitest/tsx) executes the CLI fine and the project deliberately has no `@types/node`, the cleanest path that keeps `tsc --noEmit` green without altering dependencies is a small ambient declaration. But the most standard, correct approach for a Node CLI is `@types/node`. Let me check if it can be installed, and whether tsx already bundles node types.

content.text:
The exercise is complete. Here's a summary of what was built following the mandated TDD workflow (using the `test-list`, `red`, `green`, and `refactor` agents for every phase, autonomously per experiment mode):

## What was implemented

**Core domain logic — `src/claim-office.ts`** (38 tests)
- `quote(items, context)` — premium calculation: base premiums per item type, component building-block pricing (exactly 3 alike → 60 G), item-specific surcharges (curse +50%, high-enchantment ≥5 +30%), and policy-wide modifiers (loyalty −20%, first-insurance +10%, follow-up −15%) computed against the **unmodified base premium total**, plus the 5 G fee, rounded up.
- `claim(policy, incident, remainingCapBefore?)` — payout: per-damage 100 G deductible, high-enchantment-≥8 50% rule (wins over dragon material), 2× insurance-sum cap shared across successive claims, rounded down, with validation guards (unknown/absent items, over-count, negative amounts).

**Scenario orchestration — `src/scenario.ts`** (6 tests)
- `runScenario(scenario)` — processes steps in order, flags every quote after the first as a follow-up, resolves claims to their referenced quote step by index, and threads remaining cap across successive claims on the same policy.

**CLI entry point — `src/cli.ts`**
- Reads JSON from stdin, writes `{results}` JSON to stdout; on any error exits non-zero and writes the description to stderr.

## Verification
- **44 tests pass** (38 core + 6 scenario)
- **`tsc --noEmit` clean** (added `@types/node` for the CLI's Node globals)
- End-to-end CLI confirmed against the prompt's schema example (`premium 59`, then `payout 100 / remainingCap 1100`), both integration examples (follow-up cursed sword = **160**), cap exhaustion (1400/600 → 600/0), and all four error cases (non-zero exit + stderr).

## A note on process
The TDD test list deliberately scoped out the CLI as separate integration glue. I caught that a sub-agent had written the `DONE` marker before the spec's required `src/cli.ts` existed — I removed it, drove the scenario runner through full red-green-refactor cycles, added the CLI wrapper, verified everything, and only then re-wrote the marker. Several mid-stream tests were honest false-greens (boundary confirmations) and one mid-cycle correction was significant: test 23 revealed that policy-wide percentage modifiers must apply to the *unmodified* base, not the surcharge-inclusive total — that model fix was made and the affected earlier tests' expectations updated to match the spec.

`experiment-done.txt` now contains `DONE`.
